data.title: Self-check: Is your company agent-friendly?
data.description:
Author: Zhang Feng

I. Trend: In the AI ​​Era, Comprehensive Business Integration is Unstoppable

In recent years, the global technology industry has undergone a silent but profound paradigm shift. If the past decade was the golden age of "Internet+", then the present and the next decade will be the certain future of "AI+". From the wave of large-scale models sparked by ChatGPT to the rush of various industries to deploy intelligent agents, an undeniable fact is emerging: AI is no longer a tool for adding icing on the cake, but is becoming the infrastructure for the survival and development of enterprises.

Responsibility Boundaries and Emergency Mechanisms: When the behavior of an AI agent causes losses, how should responsibility be divided? In a company's governance structure, the behavioral boundaries of the AI ​​agent, the timing of human intervention, and the post-incident handling procedures must be clearly defined.

III. Current Situation: Most Companies' Businesses Are Not Yet Ready to Connect with AI

If we examine most companies using the above four dimensions as a benchmark, we will find an embarrassing reality: companies that claim to "embrace AI" have business systems and management processes that are extremely hostile, even "antagonistic," to AI agents.

(I) From an Internal Management Perspective: The Data Swamp and the Process Maze The biggest obstacle to AI from internal management lies primarily in data quality. AI agents are not magicians; they rely on high-quality, structured, and semantically consistent data. However, the state of data within many enterprises can only be described as appalling: The same field (such as "customer name" or "product model") in different departments uses completely different naming rules and coding systems in their respective systems; Paper documents and electronic documents coexist, with a large amount of critical information locked in scanned copies, PDF images, and even audio recordings, requiring AI to perform complex OCR and speech recognition to "understand" it; Missing historical data, entry errors, and duplicate records are common, and data governance remains merely a slogan; The business processes themselves lack standardization; the same approval item may take completely different paths in different branches and with different handlers. When an AI agent attempts to access such an internal system, it faces not a clear and smooth information highway, but a data swamp full of potholes, dead ends, and dead ends. How can an AI agent that approves reimbursements automatically approve expenses if the classification logic for "travel expenses" and "transportation expenses" is inconsistent across different systems? (II) From the perspective of external business: Closed interface and arbitrary processes. External business systems also have many problems. Many companies' customer interfaces and transaction systems were not designed with the scenario of "AI agents acting as users" in mind from the outset. The lack or non-standardization of APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) is a major pain point. For example, an AI-powered procurement agent aiming to automatically compare product prices would be forced to use a "simulated browser" approach to crawl web pages if the target supplier's website lacked an open, standardized, and authenticated product information interface—a method that is fragile, inefficient, and often violates the website's terms of service. Many companies still view "external interfaces" as a technical issue rather than a strategic one, providing outdated API documentation, obsolete authentication methods, and opaque rate-limiting policies, making them extremely unfriendly to AI agents. The human-centric approach to process design is equally serious. The interfaces of most business systems are designed for human vision, human reaction time, and human attention patterns: complex multi-level menus, operation options that require "hovering" to appear, dynamically refreshed CAPTCHAs, and forced-read pop-up announcements… These designs may only be slightly inconvenient for human users, but they are insurmountable obstacles for AI agents. An AI agent aiming to automatically complete order queries will become a fragile behemoth riddled with conditional branches and exception handling when faced with a process that requires opening a three-level menu, searching for the "last 7 days" option in a vague date picker, and then encountering a satisfaction survey pop-up that may appear at any time. (III) From the perspective of ecosystem participation: fragmented efforts and lack of standards. While internal and business integration issues can be resolved through the efforts of individual companies, the challenges at the ecosystem level far exceed the control of any single enterprise. The primary problem is the heterogeneity of system architectures among enterprises. Company A's ERP and Company B's CRM have completely different underlying data models; the meanings of order status codes in one industry and logistics status codes in another are incomprehensible. When multiple intelligent agents from different enterprises attempt to collaborate on an end-to-end business process, their primary challenge is not complex business logic, but rather the fundamental problem of "translation"—a translation task that often requires extensive custom development, completely contradicting the original intention of automated collaboration. The lack or fragmentation of standards and protocols is a deeper issue. While some industry organizations and technology alliances are promoting B2B integration standards (such as certain versions of EDI, RosettaNet, OASIS, etc.), these standards are either outdated and fail to consider the autonomous interaction needs of intelligent agents; or they are too complex and costly to implement; or they have limited coverage, applicable only to specific industries or stages. Currently, an open and universal standard protocol covering the entire chain of "agent discovery - capability negotiation - data exchange - state synchronization - anomaly handling - settlement and clearing" is still lacking. Trust and security mechanisms are major obstacles to ecosystem collaboration. How can an intelligent agent from one enterprise verify the identity and authorization of another enterprise's intelligent agent? How can sensitive data during interactions be prevented from being leaked or eavesdropped on by third parties? When an intelligent agent behaves abnormally (e.g., is maliciously hijacked), how can its ecosystem access be quickly detected and severed? These problems can be partially solved under traditional API security frameworks (OAuth, API keys, mTLS, etc.), but when the interacting entity changes from an "enterprise system" to an "autonomous intelligent agent," and when the interaction mode changes from "request-response" to "multi-turn dialogue and autonomous decision-making," existing security models become inadequate. (IV) From a Governance and Compliance Perspective: Responsibility Vacuum and Regulatory Lag Compliance issues are equally worrying. Many companies, in their AI development, essentially adopt a "get it running first, then worry about the consequences" approach, with governance structures lagging significantly behind technology deployment. The attribution of responsibility for AI decisions is ambiguous—when a human resources AI agent mistakenly filters out a qualified candidate, is it the responsibility of the algorithm engineer? The business department? Or the AI ​​itself (if legally permissible)? Companies often lack clear and operational rules for assigning responsibility, ultimately resulting in no one being held accountable. The lack of ethical review mechanisms—what AI application scenarios require ethical review? Who should form the review committee? What are the review standards? Most companies simply haven't established relevant systems and processes. As a result, some AI applications with obvious risks of discrimination or privacy are launched without sufficient evaluation, only to be hastily remedied after complaints or public outcry. The "transfer mentality" of regulatory compliance—many companies directly "transfer" traditional compliance requirements to AI scenarios without understanding the new problems brought about by AI. For example, data protection regulations require users to have the right to "explain" the logic of automated decisions, but can the decision boundary of a deep neural network really be clearly "explained" in natural language? Companies often use superficial explanations such as "our model is based on three features X, Y, and Z" to evade the issue, without truly addressing the problem of explainability. IV. Action: The Inevitable Path of Comprehensive Attention Faced with the aforementioned complex situation, enterprises cannot simply passively wait for technology to mature or standards to be perfected; instead, they must proactively and systematically promote AI integration across four levels: strategy, technology, business, and compliance. (I) Strategic Level: From "Tool Thinking" to "Ecosystem Thinking" Top management needs to clearly recognize that AI is not just the next software project, but a strategic variable influencing business models and organizational structures. AI integration is not just a matter for the IT department, but for the entire company. Enterprises need to develop a clear AI strategic roadmap, specifying which business segments will introduce AI agents over the next three to five years, which agent capabilities will be developed in-house or outsourced, how to balance automation and human intervention, and how to respond to changes in the external ecosystem. More importantly, enterprises need to make a fundamental shift: from viewing AI as an internal tool for improving efficiency to viewing it as an identity carrier for participating in the external ecosystem. This means that a company's strategic planning should include considerations such as "how our agents will collaborate with agents in other organizations" and "what role we hope to play in the entire agent ecosystem." (II) Technical Aspect: Building a System Architecture "Friendly to Intelligent Agents" The technical team needs to re-examine the existing system architecture, shifting from a "human-centered" design philosophy to a "dual-center" design philosophy of "human and intelligent agent". Specifically, enterprises need to: All core business functions should be exposed through well-designed, fully documented, and version-controlled APIs, optimized for machine-to-machine call scenarios (e.g., providing batch operation interfaces, supporting asynchronous callbacks, and designing reasonable rate limiting and retry strategies). Data Readiness: Establish a unified data governance framework to ensure that key data entities (customers, products, orders, suppliers, etc.) have unique, clear, and machine-readable definitions across the company. Historical data cleaning and labeling should be treated as a strategic investment. Embedded Intelligence: Extract valuable business logic (such as approval rules, pricing strategies, and risk assessment standards) from hard-coded applications and encapsulate them into independent "capability units" that can be invoked by AI agents. This allows agents to combine these capabilities like building blocks, rather than being forced to repeatedly implement or bypass them.

Observable Design: Design sufficient logging, monitoring, and tracking capabilities for the behavior of AI agents. When multiple agents work collaboratively, it is possible to clearly trace which agent made which decision, at what time, and based on what data. This is the foundation for troubleshooting and accountability.

(III) Business Level: Redesigning Processes and Roles

Business departments cannot completely delegate AI integration to the technology department. On the contrary, the restructuring of business processes is the key to the implementation of AI.

Process Simplification and Standardization: Before integrating with AI, examine the rationality of existing processes. A process that is inherently chaotic and full of exceptions will only create more chaos after automation. Enterprises need to first streamline and standardize their business processes, reducing unnecessary branches and exceptions. Human-Machine Collaboration Model Design: Not all processes are suitable for full automation. Enterprises need to clearly define which processes will be completely handed over to AI, which require AI to process first and then human review, and which will always remain human. The key is to design smooth "transfer" and "upgrade" mechanisms, rather than simply replacing humans with AI.

Employee Empowerment: AI is not here to steal jobs, but it will certainly change the content of jobs. Enterprises need to systematically train employees, teaching them how to collaborate with AI agents, how to understand and intervene in the decision-making process of AI agents, and how to take over and handle anomalies when AI agents fail.

(IV) Compliance Level: Establishing Embedded Governance Mechanisms

Compliance should not be an afterthought, but should be embedded in the entire lifecycle of AI system design, development, deployment, and operation from day one.

Compliance should not be a post-hoc "patch", but should be embedded in the entire lifecycle of AI system design, development, deployment, and operation from day one.

data.title: a16z: Stablecoins are becoming more localized
data.description:
Authors: Robert Hackett, editor at a16z crypto; Jeremy Zhang, partner at a16z; Source: a16z crypto; Compiled by: Shaw, Jinse Finance

Stablecoins have been searching for their core value narrative for years.

Initially, they were simply a trading instrument used to transfer US dollar funds between various exchanges. Subsequently, stablecoins evolved into a savings vehicle, chosen to be held rather than used for consumption. Now, data points to a new direction: stablecoins are becoming core financial infrastructure.

The following nine charts will illustrate the underlying logic driving this trend.

1. Regulatory Implementation Boosts Market Growth For most of the stablecoin development process, regulatory uncertainty has limited institutional investment. Then, the **Genius Act** was enacted, clarifying the regulatory framework. While not the initial catalyst for this trend, the act significantly amplified the industry's growth momentum. In the United States, the **Genius Act** established the first federal regulatory framework for stablecoin issuance. This policy shift is clearly reflected in the data: even before the bill was passed, the adjusted trading volume of stablecoins had already been rising for several consecutive quarters, and the growth accelerated further after the bill was implemented, reaching approximately $4.5 trillion in the first quarter of 2026. The EU's stablecoin regulatory framework—the Crypto Asset Markets Act (MiCA)—presents a more complex picture. After the bill took full effect at the end of 2024, several major exchanges delisted Tether (USDT) for compliance, directly driving a surge in trading volume of non-USD stablecoins, exceeding $40 billion in a short period. Subsequently, trading volume of non-USD stablecoins declined and stabilized, with the overall base significantly higher than before the Crypto Asset Markets Act, and monthly volume stabilizing between $15 billion and $25 billion. Regulatory policies unexpectedly created a long-term market for non-USD stablecoins that was previously almost entirely untapped. 2. The scale of commercial applications of stablecoins continues to expand. From a long-term structural perspective, the most crucial shift may lie in the qualitative change in the actual uses of stablecoins by users. In terms of raw transaction volume, C2C (consumer-to-consumer) transactions far outpaced all other categories, reaching 789.5 million transactions in 2025. C2B (consumer-to-business) stablecoin transactions saw the fastest growth, with transaction volume increasing by 128% year-on-year, from 124.9 million transactions in 2024 to 284.6 million transactions in 2025, more than doubling in size. Data from stablecoin payment card infrastructure further confirms this trend. The monthly collateralized deposits of various stablecoin card projects (including Etherfi Cash, Kast, Wallbit, etc.) powered by Rain climbed from almost zero in November 2024 to an average of over $300 million per month in early 2026. Although this funding is used as collateral to support card spending, rather than direct stablecoin consumption, its growth momentum is remarkable: Stablecoin commercial payment scenarios are booming. 3. Stablecoin circulation velocity continues to accelerate. For every dollar of stablecoins in circulation, the frequency of fund turnover is constantly increasing. Since the beginning of 2024, the **velocity of stablecoin circulation** (the adjusted ratio of monthly transaction volume to circulating supply) has roughly doubled, climbing from 2.6 times to 6 times. This increased velocity of circulation means that the growth rate of stablecoin transaction demand has exceeded the growth rate of new issuance, significantly improving the utilization efficiency of existing funds. This is a typical characteristic of mature payment networks: the underlying currency is widely used in practice, rather than simply held passively. 4. The proportion of payment attributes in stablecoin transactions continues to increase. After excluding traditional mainstream scenarios such as trading speculation, fund transfers, and internal exchanges, the actual transaction volume of cross-entity payment transactions last year reached approximately $350 billion to $550 billion. Business-to-business (B2B) transactions dominate the stablecoin payment volume (this result is not surprising given the scale of the business). However, other scenarios such as peer-to-peer (C2C) direct transfers and merchant payments are also expanding rapidly. 5. Stablecoin payments are currently highly concentrated in specific regions. From a geographical perspective, stablecoin payment activities exhibit a clear regional imbalance. Nearly two-thirds of the transaction volume comes from Asia, mainly concentrated in Singapore, Hong Kong, and Japan. North America accounts for about a quarter, and Europe accounts for about 13%. Latin America and Africa combined have a very small share, with a transaction volume of less than $1 billion. 6. More Than Just Cross-Border Payments – Local Currencies, Global Settlement Networks The rise of non-dollar stablecoins is not unique to Europe; emerging markets are also showing significant momentum, with different driving factors behind each. Brazil is a prime example. **BRLA is a stablecoin backed by the Brazilian Real reserve.** Its monthly transaction volume grew from almost zero at the beginning of 2023 to approximately $400 million per month at the beginning of 2026. The stablecoin has been integrated with Brazil's instant payment system, PIX, significantly promoting its widespread adoption. While stablecoins are often seen as a cross-border tool, the proportion of cross-border transactions has actually been **continuously declining**, rather than increasing. The proportion of domestic transactions climbed from about half of the total payment volume in early 2024 to nearly three-quarters in early 2026. What does this mean? The development positioning of stablecoins is changing: they are no longer limited to remittance and foreign exchange tools, but are gradually becoming local daily payment mediums based on global underlying infrastructure. Conclusion: Based on various data, a clear industry picture has emerged, which is quite different from the common predictions of the public: in the past, most people believed that the core value of stablecoins would focus on cross-border transfers. But the reality is quite the opposite; stablecoins are accelerating their localization. Currently, the US dollar remains the pegged fiat currency for the vast majority of stablecoins, holding an absolute dominant position, but stablecoins are not simply products of US dollar exports. Stablecoins using non-US dollar fiat currencies such as the euro and the Brazilian real are rapidly gaining market share. Furthermore, while the number of peer-to-peer stablecoin transactions still far exceeds that of other payment types, their usage frequency in daily commercial consumption scenarios is steadily increasing. Quarterly data consistently confirms that stablecoins are gradually evolving into a universal payment infrastructure. While possessing global attributes in their underlying design, their actual application scenarios are becoming increasingly localized. The industry is still in its early stages of development, but the outline of this new financial system is becoming increasingly clear.

data.title: Goldman Sachs: What does DeepSeek V4 mean for AI in China?
data.description:
Author: Bao Yilong, Wall Street Insights

Goldman Sachs believes thatThe core significance of DeepSeek V4 lies in supporting the deployment of more complex intelligent agent applications at a lower cost, thereby opening up new space for the large-scale application of AI. On April 24th, Goldman Sachs' Ronald Keung team published a research report stating that the newly open-source V4 model is a continuation of DeepSeek's efficiency-first, open-source approach. Technically, V4 achieves significant cost reductions in long context windows through architectural upgrades and clearly bets on Huawei's domestically produced chips. In the market, this release accelerates the fierce competition in China's AI model market, with programming ability, task completion rate, and multimodal capabilities becoming the core watershed for pricing power. Goldman Sachs maintains its "Buy" rating on the Cloud Computing and Data Center sectors. Continued improvements in computing cost efficiency will accelerate the penetration of AI applications. The dual drivers of growth in enterprise AI agents and consumer AI assistants will support the continued improvement of cloud service pricing capabilities. DeepSeek V4 is upgraded with a more robust architecture, supporting longer contexts with less memory. It is released in Pro and Flash versions. The Pro version is flagship-level, with 1.6 trillion parameters (49 billion activated); the Flash version is relatively lightweight, with 284 billion parameters (13 billion activated). Both models support ultra-long context windows of 1 million tokens, on par with top-tier (SOTA) models in the US, but with significantly reduced memory requirements and key-value (KV) cache. According to a Goldman Sachs report, in a 1 million context scenario, V4 Pro requires only 27% of the floating-point operations (FLOPs) for token inference compared to DeepSeek V3.2, and its KV cache usage is only 10%; V4 Flash is even more aggressive, reducing FLOPs to 10% and compressing the KV cache to 7%. This efficiency leap is achieved through three key architectural innovations: Regarding the hybrid attention mechanism, V4 introduces a hybrid architecture of Compressed Sparse Attention (CSA) and Heavily Compressed Attention (HCA). CSA first compresses the sequence dimension of the KV cache before performing sparse attention computation, while HCA uses more aggressive compression but retains dense attention. Together, they significantly reduce the temporary memory required for long inputs. Regarding training stability, V4 introduces the mHC mechanism to enhance the stability of information transmission in multi-layer networks; it also adopts Muon as the main training optimizer (with some modules retaining AdamW) to adapt to more complex network architectures than V3, improving the convergence quality of the training process. Goldman Sachs points out that the above efficiency gains are more significant for long-duration task scenarios, with typical use cases being long-cycle proxy tasks that require processing a large amount of context. It is worth noting that DeepSeek is currently still focused on basic text models, while internet giants such as Alibaba, ByteDance, and MiniMax, as well as independent model makers, tend to favor multimodal/full-modal approaches, showing a clear divergence in their AGI exploration paths. Domestic chip deployment accelerates, Huawei Ascend 950 paves the way for price reductions. Another important signal from this V4 release is that DeepSeek has explicitly included the mass production of Huawei Ascend 950 supernodes in its commercial strategy. DeepSeek expects that with large-scale supply of Huawei Ascend 950 supernodes in the second half of 2026, the API pricing of the V4 Pro version will see a significant decrease. Goldman Sachs' report indicates that this statement has a dual meaning: First, DeepSeek's cost competitiveness will be further strengthened, creating conditions for wider application; Second, against the backdrop of continued tightening of chip supply, the trend of top Chinese AI models migrating to domestic computing power has received clear endorsement from leading players. From the current pricing perspective, Goldman Sachs data shows that the V4 Pro is already competitively priced on mainstream API platforms, and this advantage is expected to further expand in the second half of 2026 as domestic computing power supply expands. The open-source release of DeepSeek V4 has quickly triggered a new round of intensive follow-up from the Chinese AI model camp. According to Goldman Sachs, players recently launching new models include: Kimi K2.6, Alibaba Qwen3.6-Max, Tencent Hy3 preview version, Xiaomi V2.5, and MiniMax M3/Hailuo, which is expected to be launched in May. According to Goldman Sachs, the key differentiating factors that will determine the pricing power of various models in the future will focus on two dimensions: programming/task completion success rate, where Zhipu's GLM model ranks among the top in coding ability; and multimodal capabilities, where ByteDance, Alibaba, and MiniMax have invested the most deeply. The research report points out that the advantages and disadvantages of two types of players are clear: Independent AI players, such as MiniMax, have high organizational efficiency and short decision-making chains. Even with extremely low pricing for their basic text APIs, they can still achieve a 40% gross profit margin, according to Goldman Sachs' forecast. Internet giants, such as ByteDance, Tencent, and Alibaba, have abundant cash flow from their core businesses, making them more suitable for developing AI infrastructure and cloud computing. They need to set up independent incentive programs for their AI teams to retain talent; for example, ByteDance's Doubao team already has independent incentives. It is worth noting that, according to a Goldman Sachs report citing news reports, Tencent and Alibaba are in talks to invest in DeepSeek at a valuation of over $20 billion, while Zhipu and MiniMax have the latest market capitalizations of approximately $53 billion and $31 billion, respectively. This potential deal reflects the logic of giants vying for scarce top-tier AI capabilities. Goldman Sachs maintains its view that cloud computing and data centers are the preferred sub-sectors for the Chinese internet, based on the following logic: The continued growth in demand for AI tokens will drive up cloud service procurement; the growth of enterprise clients and AI agents is improving cloud/token pricing capabilities; and the continued penetration of consumer AI assistants is contributing to incremental demand. In the To-B enterprise cloud market, Alibaba leads with the largest external AI cloud revenue. In the To-C consumer market, ByteDance is currently the platform with the largest daily token usage for AI chatbots. The DAU of AIGC applications in China maintains strong growth overall, with a month-on-month growth rate of 36% in March 2026. Regarding key recommended stocks, Goldman Sachs continues to emphasize four core stocks: GDS Holdings, 21Vianet, Alibaba, and Kingsoft Cloud, as the core allocation direction to capitalize on the expansion dividends of China's AI infrastructure. In addition, the second tier consists of e-commerce and travel, the third tier consists of AI model-related stocks, and the fourth tier consists of gaming and entertainment stocks.

On April 26th, Senator Thom Tillis, who had previously stated he would block Warsh's nomination, made a concession and is now prepared to proceed with the Senate confirmation process. Tillis had previously stated firmly that he would not approve Warsh's nomination as long as the Justice Department did not cease its investigation into Powell. However, on April 24th, U.S. Attorney for the District of Columbia, Piro, announced that he would halt the investigation into Federal Reserve Chairman Powell's construction costs. Tillis's concession paves the way for the confirmation of Kevin Warsh's nomination as Federal Reserve Chair. According to PolyBeats monitoring, the probability of "Wash being confirmed as the Fed Chairman" has increased on the Polymarket forecasting market, reaching 93% by May 15th.

▌Iranian Official: Ships Passing Through the Strait of Hormuz Must Pay Fees

On April 26th, Aziz, Chairman of the National Security and Foreign Policy Committee of the Iranian Islamic Parliament, stated in an interview that all ships wishing to pass through the Strait of Hormuz must pay fees. He also stated that the Strait of Hormuz has become one of the important pillars of Iran's power. (CCTV News)

▌Iranian Spokesperson: Pakistan Lacks the Credibility Needed for Mediation

On April 27th, Ibrahim Rezaei, spokesperson for the National Security Committee of the Iranian Parliament, stated that Pakistan lacks the credibility needed for mediation. Islamabad has consistently prioritized the interests of the US President when formulating its position.

Pakistan is unwilling to acknowledge that the US has withdrawn its previously proposed solution. On April 27th, tech journalist Marc Gurman wrote that Apple's successor, John Tenus, will officially take office as CEO on September 1st, and will subsequently release the first foldable iPhone. This arrangement aims to establish Tenus as the face of the product line. The device focuses on durability and micro-crease technology, and is expected to cost over $2000. In addition to the phone, Cook has also left behind approximately 10 new product development pipelines, including AI smart home devices, AR glasses, and touchscreen Macs, to help him drive Apple into an unprecedented product expansion cycle during his new term.

▌The probability of the Fed keeping interest rates unchanged this week is 100%

According to CME's "FedWatch" on April 27th: the probability of the Fed keeping interest rates unchanged in April is 100%. The probability of the Fed cutting interest rates by a cumulative 25 basis points by June is 4.7%, and the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 95.3%.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌Can Bitcoin still be called "digital gold" under geopolitical crises?

Every time there is a geopolitical crisis, gold prices rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been confirmed by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin has an asymmetry: it falls with stocks but does not rise with stocks.

data.title: Token-Maxxing: An Arms Race No One Dares to Stop
data.description:
Author: Meng Xing, Partner, 5Y Capital; Source: LatePost

We took a trip to Silicon Valley and found that even the wave-makers are almost drowning in the waves.

On the morning of March 24, 2026, I sat in the audience at YC W26 batch Demo Day. When the fifth company came on stage to pitch, I decided to stop taking notes.

It's not that it's unimportant, but I realized that what I had written down might be outdated by next month.

This batch of over one hundred companies actually do highly concentrated work: about 80% are vertical agents, such as helping lawyers organize documents, helping customer service distribute work orders, and helping HR screen resumes.

If I had seen these projects last October, I would most likely have thought, "They're quite innovative." But the problem is, the world has changed in these five months. Claude Code has transformed from a more developer-oriented tool into an interface that almost anyone can use directly. With the release of Opus 4.6, the barrier to entry for vibe coding has been drastically reduced. Those vertical agents, before establishing business barriers, can now be built by an average engineer, or even myself, in a weekend; they have lost their investment value. YC's project cycle is three months; this batch, joining in December, plus the initial screening, are essentially "good companies" selected five months ago. Five months is enough time for several paradigm shifts to occur at the current pace of AI iteration. Back in 2012, when I started my first business and received a Fly Out from Y Combinator (YC), YC was practically unrivaled in the accelerator field, and the companies they selected often represented the "next direction." But the competitive landscape has changed, and in recent years YC seems to have reversed course, gradually becoming a lagging indicator. YC's batch system, from application, screening, induction, refinement, to pitching, has been operating successfully for over a decade in the mobile internet era. But this rhythm was designed for a slower world. Returning to the venture capital industry for the past year and a half, I've been to Silicon Valley about once a quarter; the last time was last October. In the past, every time I came here, I felt that things were changing very quickly, but this "quickness" was mostly perceived on a monthly basis. This time, it has to be measured in "weeks". One evening at dinner, a friend who works in post-training casually remarked, "I've noticed that Silicon Valley is starting to fall behind itself." [Image of a token-maxxing process] Six months ago, if someone had told me that tens of thousands of engineers at Meta were all writing code using competitors' products, I would have thought they were joking. But it's true. The entire Meta team uses Claude Code. This isn't a startup, nor some experimental team; it's a trillion-dollar company. Code security is disregarded, token budgets have exploded, leaderboards are booming, and the entire Silicon Valley is pouring money into AI without regard to cost. But what happens after all that? Let's start with code security. Six months ago, this would have been unimaginable, because code is a company's core asset. How could you allow an outside company's API to access it? Meta initially thought the same way. They internally developed something called myclaw to try and solve this problem. A friend at Meta told me they created a coding product, but it was "not user-friendly, nobody used it." After that, the company had to relax its restrictions: as long as it didn't involve customer data, anyone who wanted to use Claude Code could use it. Then, various departments started holding internal meetings on "how to become an AI-native organization," conducting training and performance evaluations. Code security and usage security, which were once taken for granted, were all relegated to the back burner; efficiency was the priority. For security reasons, Google prohibits most employees from using competitor tools like Claude Code or Codex, but DeepMind is an exception; several teams responsible for the Gemini model and internal applications use Claude Code. Google itself has also made efforts: they launched the internal coding tool Antigravity, and in February of this year, they announced that approximately 50% of the company's new code was written by AI. Even so, DeepMind is still using Claude Code. A key reason DeepMind dares to do this is that Anthropic has provided them with a private deployment; after all, Anthropic's inference and training primarily run on Google Cloud TPUs, and the two companies have this foundation of trust. But Meta and other tech giants don't have this relationship; they've truly disregarded code security. Everyone is betting on the same thing: maximizing speed first. Code security is just the first flag that falls; the second is the token budget. In the several AI-native startups I spoke with at Palo Alto, an engineer's annual token budget was roughly over $200,000. The number itself isn't surprising; what's surprising is that it means the AI ​​cost consumed by a top engineer is approaching their salary. It seems like companies are using AI to cut costs through layoffs, but in reality, the total cost may not have decreased at all; they've simply replaced human costs with token costs. Meta is the most extreme example in this regard. They created an internal token consumption leaderboard: whoever uses the most tokens is listed, and those at the bottom might be laid off. Meta employees are even vying for an unofficial title called "token legend." However, at the same time, Meta has conducted two rounds of layoffs this year, totaling tens of thousands of employees. On one hand, everyone is using Claude Code to boost token consumption; on the other hand, there are large-scale layoffs. These two things are not contradictory; they are two sides of the same coin. I visited a Series C funding company, and the technical lead showed me Slack. It was full of agents running—a dozen or so Cursor agents running in parallel in the background, with a Claude Code window for scheduling. The most prevalent anxiety among programmers right now is: if I don't know what my dozen or so agents are doing before I go to sleep, I panic. But has productivity really increased by that much? Since the end of last year, many CTOs of top inference engine and database companies have excitedly told me about "100 times more engineers" and "10 times more efficiency." What used to take 60 people a year can now be done in a week by two people plus Claude Code. At first, I was excited along with them, but later I calmed down and started asking myself: Okay, if efficiency improved 100 times, did the company's revenue increase 100 times? Or did the product line expand 100 times? Surely a "100-fold" improvement can't just mean laying off a lot of people, right? I didn't get a direct answer. The truth is, a 100-fold increase in efficiency only translates to a 50% or 1-fold increase in company revenue. Where does the difference lie? Nobody can say for sure yet. "With so many tokens, the company should have undergone a genetic mutation and become a completely different kind of company. But what exactly it will become, I don't know." A founder with a background in B2B sales told me that his team of 16 people, including two salespeople, achieved a $30 million ARR from scratch in 12 months, all thanks to AI coding. You do occasionally see cases like this. But most of the time, what I see are startups building more things, but these things lack product-market fit (PMF). Silicon Valley is currently popular for using Vibe coding to try 100 approaches to see which one works, instead of just trying 10. But who will be able to seize the next development trend? It's still hard to say. One of the most striking counterexamples came from within Anthropic. I asked a friend at Anthropic, "What's the most painful scenario for you when using agents?" He said it was oncall (instant response). A typical scenario for oncall tasks is: if Claude's API suddenly becomes slow, a model inference node crashes, or a user reports an abnormal prompt output, the oncall engineer needs to quickly pinpoint the root cause of the problem, determining whether it's a code bug, a computational allocation issue, or a model malfunction, and then decide how to fix it. Anthropic is the world's leading company in coding agents; this scenario couldn't be closer to their core competency, yet their internal oncall agent still doesn't work well. This is the reality in April 2026: the steam engine has been invented, but sometimes it's not even as fast as a horse-drawn carriage. The key is that everyone knows the steam engine will eventually run faster, so everyone is throwing money at it: code security is ignored, token budgets are overflowing, and leaderboards are booming. As for when the steam engine will actually outrun a horse-drawn carriage? Nobody knows, but nobody dares to stop and wait for that day. Because the cost of stopping might be greater than burning through the wrong tokens. Moreover, token consumption is unlikely to grow linearly. This reminds me of my experience working on autonomous driving: in 2021 in Shanghai, we achieved five consecutive hours of unattended autonomous driving for the first time. At the time, it was considered a major breakthrough. Before that, the test fleet might have gradually increased to 10, 15, or 20 vehicles; but after that inflection point, it quickly reached 100 or 1000 vehicles. Today's coding agents are in a similar stage. In 2021 in Shanghai, Didi's autonomous driving achieved its first continuous 5-hour unattended driving, a milestone event for autonomous driving in China. The picture shows Meng Xing, then COO of Didi's autonomous driving company, in conversation with Sebastian Thrun, the "father of Google's self-driving car," in 2021. METR is a California-based research institution specializing in evaluating AI coding capabilities. Last year, they proposed a metric: measuring how long an AI agent can complete a task with a 50% success rate (based on the completion time of human experts). When it was first released in March 2025, Claude 3.7 Sonnet achieved this in 50 minutes; by the end of 2025, Claude Opus 4.6 had reached 14.5 hours. Over the past two years, the doubling cycle of this metric has been compressed from 7 months to 4 months. Once the agent's reliability improves further, token consumption won't just increase by 50% annually, but will increase by an order of magnitude overnight. A widely accepted prediction is that by the end of this year, many companies (including major tech companies) will actually only need 20% of their workforce. After the xAI team collapsed, the rocket builders started building models. At a steakhouse in Mountain View, around 9 PM, a friend who had worked with Musk for a long time sat down across from me. We chatted for over three hours, and looking back, I realized he hadn't said a single good word about Musk. One detail: I asked him, "You've worked at xAI for three years, what's your daily routine like?" He said he'd basically lived at the company for three years, so his home wasn't very furnished, and he hadn't even bought a bed. He slept in one of those sleeping pods at the company, like in a youth hostel. I said, "Now that you have a huge amount of stock and have left, you should at least buy a bed." He smiled. xAI's workload is notoriously intense in Silicon Valley, but now about 90% of the early team has left. They have a departure group that's constantly adding new members. The trigger was Tony Wu's firing, which then triggered a chain reaction. As one insider put it, "Other companies might need six months to prepare for a senior management team leaving, but xAI only needed a month." Some people sensed Musk's dissatisfaction as early as last October, but didn't expect such a complete purge so quickly. Now Musk is bringing in people from SpaceX and Tesla to take over xAI; "the rocket builders are starting to build models." Musk's dissatisfaction stems from the fact that he poured countless funds and computing power into the project, yet Grok has never been able to reach the front lines. But why? This is a question I ask everyone I meet who comes from xAI. The answer is actually simpler than I imagined. A friend put it bluntly: the team is incredibly effective and works extremely hard, but the management style of manufacturing might not be suitable for large-scale model companies. Having worked in autonomous driving for eight years, I have some insights into this. Musk's past work with SpaceX and Tesla was essentially systems engineering: a long chain involving software, hardware, and the supply chain, each with room for innovation, but ultimately an end-to-end engineering problem. He excels at identifying key leverage points within this long chain and then compressing the timeline to overcome them. Rocket engine cascading and reusable landing are products of this thinking. But at xAI, what he does doesn't seem like systems engineering. He's doing three things now: first, building the world's largest GPU cluster (today, people jokingly say that xAI, originally a neo lab, is now more like a neo cloud, providing computing power to Cursor), then setting pulse-like deadlines for the team, and personally filming some product features. This is focusing on a few key points, not making a comprehensive plan. Those working in autonomous driving know that in the later stages, the core conflict becomes "who leads whom" among the software, infrastructure, and hardware teams. All three directions require CTO-level decision-making, but no one understands all three areas simultaneously. A better approach is for the founder, while not an expert in every area, to know how to balance resources and determine phased priorities—software first in one phase, then infrastructure in the next. This is called having a holistic plan. xAI's problem is the lack of this holistic plan; it's all about sprinting. If the pressure isn't too intense, intelligent people can actually self-correct; given time, each direction will find its own rhythm of collaboration. But Musk's extremely high-pressure management, coupled with insufficient overall planning, caused everything to fall apart under pressure. Each person in charge of each direction was prioritizing their own priorities; no one was coordinating the overall picture. One overlooked reason for SpaceX and Tesla's success is that Musk has essentially never encountered competitors of equal scale in these two industries; he's been competing against himself. But AI is different. AI is a fiercely competitive field where even OpenAI could be undermined by Anthropic. A co-founder of xAI said last year that there were two things he hadn't anticipated: first, the competition was so fierce; second, the opportunities for application innovation in the AI ​​era were so few, all being consumed by models. The rise of Anthropic represents the most dramatic reversal in the AI ​​industry over the past year. It has also completely changed the focus of the battle: a year ago, everyone was vying for C-end user numbers and video generation; now, the (temporarily) decisive battleground is B2B and coding. Of course, the xAI story is also a story of "what happens when money comes too fast and too much." I think those who left xAI today won't regret their decision to join; xAI is arguably the fastest wealth-creation myth in Silicon Valley. xAI went from raising billions of dollars in its first round of funding to becoming a $250 billion behemoth after merging with SpaceX, all in just one year. Almost every one of xAI's nine co-founders has become a billionaire, and its core engineers have earned tens of millions to hundreds of millions of dollars. Silicon Valley truly has too much money. If they were to start another business today, they would have the confidence to pursue their interests rather than chasing quick profits. Conversations with engineers reveal a strange unspoken understanding: everyone admits they don't write much code anymore, but pretends it's no big deal because they'll become AI-armed, eliminating those engineers who haven't embraced AI. Today, 80% of the core skills of software engineers have already been replaced by models. The only reason they're still around is because models occasionally make mistakes and require human supervision. But the act of "supervising" itself may soon be unnecessary. To take a more radical view: today's so-called "AI native organizations" sound very sexy—having each department streamline workflows, digitize AI-enabled components, and write them as skills. But essentially, it's about manually distilling oneself: you turn your abilities into machine skills, the company acquires your skills, and has effectively completed AI transformation. Whether to lay off employees because of this is a moral question. Meta is doing just that today. Although everyone is talking about token-maxxing today, you can still feel a pervasive sense of anxiety at the bottom of Silicon Valley. What surprised me even more was that this anxiety was spreading to the researcher community. Researchers are the elite at the very top of the pyramid. It doesn't refer to any general "researcher," but rather to those at large modeling companies (OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepMind, etc.) who are responsible for model training and algorithm innovation. The difference between them and engineers is: engineers "build things," writing code, deploying, and optimizing performance; researchers are further upstream, "thinking about what to build": proposing new training methods, designing model architectures, and running experiments to verify hypotheses. And now, even the work of researchers is being automated. This is what DeepMind is doing—using models to train models, which is also the AI ​​self-evolution that's been all the rage in Silicon Valley this year. This year, engineers are being phased out, and by the end of the year, researchers will also begin to be replaced. This isn't a new concept. Andrej Karpathy's auto research pioneered this approach, and today various AI scientist tools and harness frameworks are moving in this direction. However, most current closed loops only reach the "paper publication" level—AI runs experiments and writes papers for you, but ultimately, humans still make the judgments. Companies like OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google are aiming for something more radical: they hope the closed loop will extend directly to model upgrades themselves, not just minor improvements, but allowing AI to find the next paradigm-level breakthrough on its own. If this can be achieved, it will truly replace researchers. Google DeepMind has been working on this internally for over a year, letting the model decide what experiments to run next, evaluate which path is more promising, and then follow that path—this is how the model trains its next generation. Moreover, researchers have a greater incentive to be laid off, for a brutal reason—because they are expensive. There may only be a few thousand researchers globally, with annual salaries often reaching millions, tens of millions, or even hundreds of millions of dollars. "The future scenario might be that 10 people do the work of 100 people, receive 20 salaries, and then 90 people lose their jobs." And the actual number of layoffs is much larger than the reported figures. Many companies are not cutting their own financial statements first, but rather their outsourcing service providers. This means that countries like India and the Philippines, which once provided customer service, data labeling, and financial back-office support to Europe and the US, may be the first to be impacted. The "service industry ladder" that some developing countries rely on to upgrade their economies may be being ripped away by AI. The entire Silicon Valley is watching Meta closely. If its experiment succeeds—revenue doesn't drop and efficiency truly improves—other large companies will quickly follow suit, and layoffs will go from isolated cases to industry norms. Moreover, layoffs have a cruel self-accelerating mechanism: initially, people are hesitant to lay off employees for fear of damaging morale; once it becomes the norm, layoffs happen faster and faster, with less concern for the consequences. However, while old positions are being eliminated, new ones are emerging. Many startups are starting to hire a new role called "AI builder"—combining the roles of product manager, front-end engineer, and back-end engineer. There are also hybrid roles combining data scientist and machine learning engineer, as well as content operators who integrate writing, advertising, and operations. Silicon Valley companies have a huge demand for these new roles, but the core problem is: nobody knows how to recruit them. You can't screen them out with resumes because the role didn't exist before, and the person's abilities might all be hidden in their own projects; you can't test them by having them write code on the spot because the core competency is a combination of "aesthetic sense + AI usage ability." So some startups are already doing this: automatically generating a simulated environment based on the employer's needs, allowing interviewees to complete tasks on the spot using AI tools. It's a bit like the old coding tests, but it tests something entirely new. When AI can do everything, the value of humans is shifting from "what they can do" to judging "what is worth doing and what shouldn't be done." One round of financing, two valuations: Nvidia wants to grab chips at every "table". We've talked about so many people being replaced—engineers, researchers, and financial professionals. But one role hasn't been replaced; instead, it's become increasingly like the behind-the-scenes boss in this reshuffling. This seemingly distributed world of innovation is actually extremely centralized at its core. That center is Nvidia. I originally thought the scarcity of GPUs had eased in the past year. There was indeed a period of relative calm. Around mid-2025, some neoclouds (new cloud service providers specializing in GPU computing power that emerged during the AI ​​wave), backed by Nvidia, struggled with fundraising, some experienced sluggish business growth, and some even sold themselves at that time. But this time, I found that scarcity has returned, and it's even more outrageous than before. A concrete signal: if you can stably provide an API service today, like Claude's API, achieving 99th percentile stability, you can sell it for two to three times the price of the official API. Following the surge in demand for Anthropic, API outages are becoming more frequent, posing a problem for many Agent products built on Claude. Previously, the business of providing Router services was based on the principle of "I'm cheaper than the official provider, so I get traffic." Now the logic is completely reversed: stability itself has become a scarce resource. A number of startups have made a lot of money from this, and mini versions of Coreweave/Nebius are springing up like mushrooms in Silicon Valley. Moreover, this time the computing bottleneck is not just a matter of GPU allocation. Elad Gil recently wrote an assessment I strongly agree with: the capacity expansion cycle for upstream memory manufacturers (Hynix, Samsung, Micron) will take at least another two years. This means that before 2028, no AI company can significantly differentiate itself by simply increasing computing power. Computing power constraints are objectively reinforcing the oligopoly in the large-scale model market—it's not that anyone isn't working hard, it's just that the manufacturing cycle in the physical world is that slow. The underlying power structure is clear: whoever has the cards is powerful, and who has the cards is determined by Nvidia. CoreWeave, Lambda, and Nebius, which went public today, all have Nvidia backing them. Nvidia's strategy is deeper than I previously understood. An investor in Reflection told me that when Neo Lab first came out for funding, it was doing coding. Then the founder met with Jensen Huang, who told him: "Stop coding, come out and create 'the American DeepSeek,' an open-source model for the US. I'll give you money and resources." Reflection then did a 180-degree turn. This led to some previously unseen structures in the US capital market: in the same funding round, two valuation tiers were offered. Investors with good relationships and early entry went into the lower valuation tier; established giants like Nvidia, with their deep pockets, and later investors were squeezed into the higher valuation tier. This structure has recently started to appear in China as well. But no matter how much Nvidia tries to control the allocation, it can't control something that doesn't exist. Protests against data centers are escalating across American society. Currently, approximately 100 data center projects nationwide are facing obstacles, with 40 likely to be cancelled altogether. Maine recently passed a bill completely banning data center construction. One town approved a $6 billion data center project, only to have half of its members voted to rescind it overnight; the sole purpose of the new leadership was to reverse the decision. The problem isn't a lack of computing power, not because products are bad or users are insufficient, but because the physical world can't keep up with the demands of the digital world. This is another level of "not keeping up." Silicon Valley's valuation system is being rewritten. Let's look at a number. The US GDP is approximately $30 trillion. OpenAI and Anthropic currently have annualized revenue run rates (ARRs) of around $30 billion each, meaning each company already accounts for 0.1% of the US GDP. If both reach $100 billion by the end of the year, plus cloud services and other AI revenue, AI will account for approximately 1% of the US GDP. From almost zero to 1%, it only took a few short years. This speed is unprecedented. But strangely, the faster the growth, the less investors know how to price the company—the valuation framework in Silicon Valley is collapsing in the face of such rapid growth. I had several in-depth conversations with friends in the secondary market, and one recurring term was "re-rationalization." In the past few years, the valuation logic for AI investments was based on future cash flows: it doesn't matter if you lose money today, I'm betting on your ARR (Average Return) three or five years from now. But now, this framework is flawed. The problem lies in the most basic valuation model: DCF (Discounted Cash Flow). Normally, with DCF, you predict cash flows for the next 10 years and then add a terminal value, which is the assumption that the company will continue to operate stably, packaging the remaining value in one lump sum. Typically, the terminal value accounts for 70%-80% of the total valuation. But two things have changed simultaneously: First, you can probably only predict 3 years instead of 10, because it's impossible to see what the industry will look like in 3 years (sometimes even 1 year); second, terminal value is even more impossible to calculate. It assumes the company will eventually operate stably, but if AI could disrupt everything at any time, the assumption of "stable operation" no longer holds. I discussed an analogy with a friend who works in secondary investment: companies not on the AI ​​front today are more like waiting for a "nuclear bomb"—you know they will be disrupted, just not when. Therefore, your evaluation focus shouldn't be "what if they aren't disrupted," but rather "how quickly they can respond when disrupted." This is a completely different valuation logic. SaaS was the first to be repriced by Wall Street. In 2023, Snowflake's return on investment, calculated based on free cash flow, would have taken nearly 100 years. Now, its valuation has been halved, and ServiceNow and Workday are following the same trend—this is just the beginning. Conversely, the only companies truly suitable for DCF valuation might be the leading large-scale companies, as their future seems to be steadily growing in a positive direction. They won't be "bombed," but rather focused on expanding their boundaries. In the past, startups would advertise "lower salaries, but stock options; they'll be worth a lot in the future." However, this approach presupposes that the company will still be around and valuable in 15 to 20 years. If that premise no longer holds, the most rational reaction from employees will be—"Don't give me stock options, just give me cash." This, in turn, will change the company's cost structure and financing logic. VCs are also suffering. In the past 3 to 6 months, almost every fund in Silicon Valley has invested in at least one neo lab, researchers from renowned AI labs who raised hundreds of millions of dollars with their ideas. But now, everyone feels it was a bit impulsive and expensive. But why did they still invest? Because if this company actually succeeds, its growth will be so fast that you'll feel the initial valuation was cheap. An investor friend put it bluntly: it's either zero to 100 or zero to zero anyway; rather than investing in an expensive Series A round and earning "hard-earned money," it's better to gamble on a ticket to a neo lab with unlimited potential. In the past, everyone thought that a $1 ARR meant exactly that, regardless of whether you were building models, applications, or infrastructure. But now, that equation has been broken. Vertical agents have the lowest ARR (around 5x), general agents have higher ARR (around 10x), and models have the highest (20-30x ARR, for example, Anthropic's $30 billion ARR, $800 billion valuation, 26.7x). A year ago, I thought multiplying the ARR by a uniform factor to calculate the valuation was sufficient, but today that method is completely wrong. Lime Trees and AI Assassination Lists Silicon Valley is experiencing a deep-seated security crisis. During my recent trip to Silicon Valley, I repeatedly heard friends seriously discussing the same thing: buying Bitcoin, building bunkers, and installing bulletproof glass in their homes—and they weren't joking. Recently, planting lime trees has indeed become popular in Silicon Valley because these trees have 4-inch thorns on their branches, and anyone attempting to climb over them will pay a price. The Wall Street Journal even reported on a $15 million "fortress mansion": a ring of lime trees planted in concrete flowerpots, behind which was a moat, and behind the moat was a laser intrusion detection system. The front door was a 3-inch thick solid steel plate with 13 bolts, and inside was a secure refuge room with a 2,000-pound heavy door. Even the landscaping was a defensive fortification. Companies providing residential security for CEOs saw their highest growth since 2003. This trend accelerated sharply, especially after the shooting death of UNH's CEO in Manhattan. Then, gunshots rang out at the doorstep of an AI mogul. At 4 a.m. on April 11, a 20-year-old boy wearing a Champion hoodie flew from Texas to California, carrying a kerosene can, stood in front of Sam Altman's $27 million mansion, lit a Molotov cocktail, and threw it inside. An hour and a half later, he appeared at OpenAI headquarters, grabbed a chair, smashed the glass door, and yelled at security, "I'm going to burn this place down and kill everyone inside." The FBI found a document on him titled "Your Final Warning." It listed the names and home addresses of several AI company CEOs and investors. Two days later, early Sunday morning, Altman's home was attacked again: a Honda sedan briefly stopped at the door, the passenger stuck his hand out the window, fired a shot into the house, and then fled. This was not an isolated incident. In late March, a large-scale anti-AI protest had already taken place in downtown San Francisco, with crowds holding signs that read "Stop the AI ​​Race" and "Don't Build Skynet," and giving speeches outside the offices of Anthropic, OpenAI, and xAI. Senator Bernie Sanders warned in Congress, "Humanity may really lose control of this planet." Talking with friends at xAI, it's said that Musk is also very worried about being shot, which is an open secret in the industry. The underlying fear is actually quite simple: if AI takes over most of production and humans are no longer essential participants in the economy, then all past social contracts regarding "how much you contribute and how much you should receive" become invalid. What remains is a minimalist power structure: whoever controls the GPUs and electricity controls everything. The social hierarchy isn't just widened, it's flattened: on one side, a tiny minority; on the other, everyone else. "Two years from now, the hottest campaign theme in the US presidential election will definitely be the relationship between AI and society. There might even be a Luddite movement in the AI ​​era." (Image of a PNG file: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7456285_image3.png) Inflation in the US remains severe. I've lived in California for many years and have never seen gas prices in the 7s. Just then, Citrini released its Global Intelligence Crisis report at the end of February, which simulated an economic crisis that could occur in 2028 due to AI's "over-success"... On the plane back to Beijing, I flipped through my notes from the past two weeks and realized that I had been writing the same word the entire time: "out of touch." YC couldn't keep up, Meta's code security guidelines couldn't keep up, xAI's management couldn't keep up, researchers couldn't keep up, computing power couldn't keep up, valuation frameworks couldn't keep up, and even society's psychological resilience couldn't keep up... to the point that Silicon Valley itself couldn't keep up with itself. But finally, I want to say that a friend from Anthropic mentioned that Dario Amodei said internally: "With the help of AI, cancer has been conquered in a sense. It doesn't mean it has disappeared, but rather that it may become a chronic disease that doesn't kill people. It's just that treatment costs are still too high, and widespread adoption will take time." I'm not sure if Dario's statement that "cancer has been conquered" is overly optimistic, but during my time in Silicon Valley, the most common startup direction we saw was AI4S and AI for Biotech. Many people from large modeling companies don't understand medicine, but they want to use AI technology to change the industry. In the past two weeks, I've seen so many companies "falling behind," which is indeed anxiety-inducing. But if AI truly makes cancer a chronic disease within a few years and accelerates materials science by twenty years, then this "falling behind" might be the biggest acceleration in human history. My child is two years old this year, and we might have a second child next year. I have absolutely no imagination to imagine what the world their generation will face will be like. But I hope that in the world they grow up in, there will be more people cured by AI, rather than more Molotov cocktails and gunshots raining down on the doorsteps of AI practitioners. In his 2008 book, *Cities and Ambition*, Paul Graham wrote: “While people in Silicon Valley have great respect for intelligence, the message Silicon Valley sends is: you should be more influential, which is not entirely the same message it sends in New York. Influence is certainly important in New York, but New York highly values ​​‘billions of dollars,’ even if you just inherited that money. But in Silicon Valley, apart from a few real estate agents, nobody cares about that. What really matters in Silicon Valley is how much impact you have on the world. People care about Larry and Sergey not because of their wealth, but because they control Google, and Google affects almost everyone.” Now, AI has pushed this atmosphere to a new level. LatePost columnist Meng Xing: Partner at Five Sources Capital and former COO of Didi Autonomous Driving. This is his first article in his AI investment observations; he will continue to update his investment observations on LatePost.

data.title: Which is more suitable for you, OpenClaw or Hermes?
data.description:
Author: @kloss_xyz; Translator: Peggy, BlockBeats

If 2025 was the year of the "large model capability race," then at the beginning of 2026, the focus of the competition has clearly shifted to another more concrete and realistic track—how to truly implement personal AI agents.

This article systematically compares the two most watched projects in the current AI Agent field—OpenClaw and Hermes Agent. The former has accumulated an astonishing community size and developer ecosystem in a short period of time, becoming a phenomenal AI project on GitHub; the latter, on the other hand, has rapidly entered the market with a path of "lower cost, lower barriers to entry, and stronger self-learning capabilities," and has begun to overtake in search popularity and user migration.

In fact, the difference between the two is not at the functional level, but in their design philosophy.

One path emphasizes control and flexibility, with users personally building, scheduling models, and orchestrating skills; the other emphasizes automation and efficiency, with systems learning on their own, reducing costs, and lowering the barrier to entry. This differentiation bears a striking resemblance to the Windows vs. Mac competition of the PC era, and even earlier software tool stratification: it's not about one replacing the other, but rather different user groups making different trade-offs regarding "efficiency, control, and cost." In this sense, the competition between OpenClaw and Hermes is essentially answering a longer-term question: will AI agents become "programmable personal operating systems" or "self-evolving work agents"? As model capabilities gradually converge, the real dividing line is shifting from "who is smarter" to "who is easier to use, cheaper, and closer to real-world workflows." The value of this article lies in its attempt to penetrate emotional biases, return to the structure itself, and analyze this crucial competition that has yet to be fully explained. The following is the original text: In early 2026, OpenClaw accomplished something no software project had ever done before. It garnered 346,000 stars on GitHub—surpassing React's total accumulated over ten years in less than five months. It became the most-starred AI project in GitHub history. It boasted 38 million monthly visitors and 500,000 running instances globally. For a few months, if you were in the AI ​​agent field, OpenClaw was the only topic of conversation, while Anthropic firmly held the top spot. Then, the tide turned. In March, Hermes Agent—developed by Nous Research—entered GitHub Trending. Search trends began to shift. By April, Hermes had surpassed OpenClaw in Google searches for the agent category. The project that had dominated this sector for months was now watching helplessly as a new challenger eroded its search traffic. Now, everyone has an opinion. Most opinions are either staunch OpenClaw supporters or Hermes enthusiasts—but no one is truly explaining the essential differences between the two. So, I'll do an honest breakdown and comparison so everyone can see what's really going on behind the noise. First, what are they?

OpenClaw

OpenClaw is a personal AI agent that runs on your local machine. It connects to your messaging channels, manages contexts across sessions, and performs tasks through skills. You can invoke any model through it—Anthropic's Claude (Opus, Sonnet), OpenAI's GPT-5.5, Kimi K2.6, Grok, and more.

It integrates with Claude Code for handling heavy programming tasks. Think of it as a persistent brain residing on your hardware, knowing your full configuration, and able to run 24/7 in the background—connected to every tool and channel you use.

Hermes Agent, developed by Nous Research, is also a locally running personal AI agent—but with a completely different philosophy. You don't need to write skills or configure everything yourself; Hermes learns on its own. Every task it completes is distilled into reusable knowledge. Over time, it becomes increasingly adept at handling your specific workflows without you actively telling it to. It has over 40 built-in tools and its operating cost for equivalent tasks is far lower than OpenClaw. Both address the same problem: giving you an AI agent running on your own hardware, not someone else's server. But their philosophies leading to this goal are entirely different. This is precisely what makes this debate interesting. The question isn't which is better, but which philosophy suits you better. Like the Windows vs. Apple debate. Both offer similar functionality and run on your hardware, but attract vastly different users. Windows appeals to developers and gamers seeking control and customization; Apple attracts designers and entrepreneurs looking for out-of-the-box functionality. There's no right or wrong; they cater to different people and have different priorities. Analogy: Ferrari vs. Honda. The most accurate summary of the differences between these two comes from @garrytan. @garrytan Using OpenClaw is basically like driving a Ferrari that you have to mechanic yourself—it's always breaking down, but the experience is amazing; Hermes Agent is a reliable Honda; and Claude/ChatGPT is like taking the bus. That's it. That's the real difference. OpenClaw gives you more performance and more customization—but you still have to mechanic yourself. Hermes is more stable out of the box, has lower operating costs, and is easier to learn. There's no right or wrong; they're designed for different drivers. The Advantages of OpenClaw Skill Ecosystem OpenClaw has the most mature skills market in the field. The official ClawHub catalog boasts over 44,000 skills—all of which undergo security audits before being uploaded, ensuring they are free of malware and scams. Additionally, there are premium paid options like LarryBrain, offering over 100 high-quality automated skills that can be installed in seconds. The community has been deeply involved in OpenClaw for a longer period, and its accumulated expertise is evident. Hermes is catching up quickly, but hasn't reached that level yet. Model Flexibility This is one of OpenClaw's biggest advantages, yet it's often overlooked. You're not locked into a single provider. Anthropic, OpenAI, Kimi, Grok, and local models running through Ollam—you can choose the most suitable model for each task. Use Opus models for strategy, Sonnet workers for execution, and GPT-5.5 for task-specific tasks—all within the same configuration. This flexibility is a true competitive advantage. Channel Integration: OpenClaw supports integration with more platforms such as Telegram, Discord, WhatsApp, iMessage, and Slack. Your agent exists across messaging channels, handling tasks on multiple platforms. Hermes has very limited channel support in comparison—this is where OpenClaw clearly excels. Multi-Agent Architecture: OpenClaw natively supports running multiple dedicated agents with different roles, models, and task-specific sub-agents. The sub-agent system is built-in and mature. Community, Documentation, and Endorsements: OpenClaw started earlier. Its community is much larger, with 38 million monthly visitors and 500,000 running instances. Its documentation is also more complete. It's worth noting that the original author, steipete, was recruited by OpenAI, which brought more contributors and resources to the project. When problems arise—and inevitably do—more people have already encountered the same pitfalls and fixed the same issues. @Paul_Beauchemin I use both. Hermes accomplished nothing in three weeks, while OpenClaw performs tasks every day. The Advantage of Hermes Self-Improvement Cycle This is what's truly exciting about Hermes—and the core of its philosophy that distinguishes it from all other products. With each completed task, it extracts effective methods and stores them as reusable skills. Your agent becomes increasingly proficient in your specific workflow without you doing anything. OpenClaw also has memory and skills, but you have to build them manually. Hermes builds them itself. Over time, this difference accumulates compounded into something meaningful. Token Costs: This data is hard to ignore. One founder reported that for the same task, he spent $130 on OpenClaw in 5 days, but only $10 on Hermes—and with better results. It's important to note that the cost difference depends on the models used by each platform—but Hermes is designed with cost efficiency as a core principle. If your API bills are giving you a headache, this is a major reason why people switch to Hermes. Out of the Box: Hermes comes pre-installed with over 40 working tools—Notes, iMessage, a browser, image generation, scheduled tasks, Obsidian integration. It's ready to use right out of the box. OpenClaw gives you a blank canvas. That blank canvas is powerful—but it can take weeks to create anything truly impressive. For most people, this hurdle is the real reason they don't use it. Hermes completely eliminates this barrier. Hermes runs tasks in isolated environments. Each task is independent and self-contained, without interfering with others. This is a substantial security advantage for those running sensitive workflows—customer data, financial tasks, anything you want to manage in sections. @TeancumsRaiders I spent a month working on OpenClaw, then a week using Hermes simultaneously. I struggled with whether to abandon all my previous work, but ultimately realized I was falling into the sunk cost fallacy and switched completely. I've never looked back, and I've never regretted it. Honest Comparison

OpenClaw

·High configuration complexity—you build, you control

·High cost of unboxing tokens (depending on the model used)

·Large skill marketplace—44,000+ free skills on ClawHub, with paid options

·Self-improvement is manual—you need to write or download skills yourself

·Extensive channel integration (Telegram, Discord, WhatsApp, iMessage, Slack)

·Can run any model—Anthropic, OpenAI, Kimi, Grok, run local models via Ollama

·Native multi-functionality

data.title: Cryptocurrency pyramid schemes have been busted – the projects involved typically fall into these four categories.
data.description:
In recent years, the number of virtual currency-related cases investigated and prosecuted by public security organs for the crime of organizing and leading pyramid schemes has increased significantly. In many such cases we have handled, the projects involved by our clients have different names and models, but if we break down and classify these project models, we can see that their underlying principles are highly repetitive.

Based on domestic judicial cases that have already been adjudicated, Attorney Shao has broadly summarized virtual currency pyramid scheme cases into the following four typical structures. Clarifying which model the project in question belongs to is the prerequisite for all subsequent work—different characterizations lead to completely different defense approaches.

1. Disguised as "financial wallets/quantitative tools"

Simply put, it's a platform that tells you: deposit your coins, and I'll automatically "arbitrage" for you, giving you daily returns.

The projects involved usually disguise themselves as decentralized wallets, quantitative trading robots, or digital asset value-added service platforms, claiming to possess technical capabilities such as "intelligent arbitrage," "AI arbitrage," and "cross-chain exchange," promising users high fixed returns, ranging from 10% to 60% per month, simply by depositing designated virtual currencies.
